coredata.dc:title: Enhanced green H2 generation using biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots incorporated with rare earth metal hydroxide [Gd(OH)3] nanorods
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraju, Nithya
Sivalingam, Yuvaraj
Venugopal, Gunasekaran
coredata.dc:description: Metal support interaction with carbonaceous electrodes as a heterogeneous catalyst is a trendy study area for boosting electrocatalysis with better conductivity, outstanding stability, and faster kinetics. By harnessing the power of defects caused by hetero-atom doping, interfacial effects, spatial confinement with a metal hydroxide, and the combination of two different dimensional materials, n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 exhibits an overpotential of 0.062 V with Tafel slope of 65 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2 of current density, as well as a longer hydrogen evolution reaction endurance of 25 h. We attempted to relate it to heterogeneous material activity descriptors like surface potential and work function using a Scanning Kelvin Probe. This effect results in a 200-times reduction in resistance, particularly a 9.7 Ω reduction for 0.007 V change in charge transfer resistance. This study demonstrates that overcoming the primary challenges and enhancement of green hydrogen production can be achievable by rare earth metal combinations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalyst
n-CDs
Volmer
Heyrovsky
Gadolinium
Hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of n-CDs at different magnification (a, b) (Inset: SAED pattern (a), particle size distribution (b)), lattice fringes (C), HRTEM images of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 at different magnification (d, e) (Inset: SAED pattern(d) lattice fringes(e) with FFT diffraction pattern using image J).
EDS spectrum of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 and elemental composition.
XRD pattern of (a) n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, (b) n-CDs, and (c) Gd(OH)3 (assigned with JCPDS No.83–2037).
(a) XPS survey spectra of n-CDs, Gd(OH)3, and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, (b) Elemental composition of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3.
De-convoluted (a) C 1s spectra, (b) N 1s spectra, (c) O 1s spectra, and (d) Gd 4d spectra.
FT-IR spectrum of n-CDs, Gd(OH)3 and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3.
(a) LSV spectra and (b) Tafel slope of bare GCE, n-CDs, n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, and bare Pt.
Zeta Potential of (a) n-CDs (b) Gd(OH)3 (c) n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 and (d) comparative graph.
SKP raster scanning image (a) n-CDs, (b) Gd(OH)3, and (c) n-CDs@Gd(OH)3.
Comparative XPS survey of n-CDs, and n-CDs@Gd (OH)3, [C1s spectra is shown as inset].
(a) Chrono-potentiometry @ 10 mA/cm2, and (b) LSV spectra before and after 25 h.
(a1) Nyquist plot from-0.24 to −0.74 and its corresponding Bode plots (b1) and (a2) Nyquist plot from-0.734 to −0.741 and its corresponding Bode plots (b2).
(a) CV Curves at different scan rates (b) current density vs scan rate (c) Tafel slope from fitted RCT values.
HER performance comparison of carbon quantum dots and its composites.

head.ce:title: Enhanced green H2 generation using biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots incorporated with rare earth metal hydroxide [Gd(OH)3] nanorods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, School of Technology, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Neelakudi campus, Thiruvarur, 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, School of Technology, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur, 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Sensors, Energy and Electronic Devices, Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai, 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Sensors, Energy and Electronic Devices, Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai- 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal support interaction with carbonaceous electrodes as a heterogeneous catalyst is a trendy study area for boosting electrocatalysis with better conductivity, outstanding stability, and faster kinetics. By harnessing the power of defects caused by hetero-atom doping, interfacial effects, spatial confinement with a metal hydroxide, and the combination of two different dimensional materials, n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 exhibits an overpotential of 0.062 V with Tafel slope of 65 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2 of current density, as well as a longer hydrogen evolution reaction endurance of 25 h. We attempted to relate it to heterogeneous material activity descriptors like surface potential and work function using a Scanning Kelvin Probe. This effect results in a 200-times reduction in resistance, particularly a 9.7 Ω reduction for 0.007 V change in charge transfer resistance. This study demonstrates that overcoming the primary challenges and enhancement of green hydrogen production can be achievable by rare earth metal combinations.

ce:section.ce:para: Eco-friendly environment and energy are the two key lenses of modernized society for sustainable development [1]. Renewable resources are becoming more affordable, and meeting global energy demand with zero toxic emission technology is becoming possible [2,3]. Hydrogen (H2) is a viable energy carrier in terms of environmental friendliness and high gravimetric energy density [4]. Presently major contribution of H2 production in industries is being done by reforming natural gas, which requires a substantial quantity of non-renewable energy while also emitting the greenhouse gas carbon dioxide [5]. The main consequences of greenhouse gas emissions include climate change, global warming, and acid rain caused by sulphur emissions from industries [6]. Apart from this availability of intermittent, conversion of energy and their storage had become an unavoidable and considered point. Harvesting hydrogen by electrocatalysis has been proven to be a clean and long-lasting alternative to limited fossil fuels [7]. Hydrogen has long been thought to be a good contender for energy storage [8]. Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is a half-step cathodic proton reduction in electrochemical water splitting. (HER, 2H+ + 2 e− → H2). Hydrogen is a viable vector for clean energy systems, but more efficient and scalable production is necessary before it can be widely used. For achieving large-scale production, the choice of electrode material is the crucial point. According to the Sabatier principle, the lowest over-potential and highest current density are the decisive criteria, and the reactions that occur at the surface of the catalyst are all feasible for platinum, a noble catalyst for HER [9]. Researchers are working on other materials which equalize the platinum in catalytic processes, starts from transition metals (Pd, W, Ni, and Ir) and rare earth metals [10–14]. To be an effective catalyst, a large active surface area is a key characteristic for a material that can be obtained readily from carbon and allotropes of carbon, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have that point as well as the benefit of being a zero-dimensional material surface-to-volume ratio. CQDs are plentiful, less costly, non-toxic and have a unique electron transfer capacity. They also have a large number of functional groups on their surface, which improves catalytic properties [15]. Making an amorphous catalyst, which has a disordered atomic orientation, may result in more electrochemically active sites, is one strategy for improving HER activity. Metal-supported amorphous carbon dots improve HER results by enhancing endurance and providing a broad surface area with numerous active sites, promoting electron transport kinetics. The electronic surface potential of CQDs remains negative because of the hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups initiate electrostatic interactions with metal cations that enhance the kinetics of HER [16]. The flaws caused by quantum confinement, edge effects, and, notably heteroatom doping make it a promising material for integrating HER catalysts in a single electrolyte. Rare earth metals are now being researched as attractive possibilities in the fields of optoelectronic devices, catalysis, sensors, drug delivery, and biological applications due to their superior electrical, structural, optical, and physicochemical responses resulting from electrons. They are often more crystalline and have more defects in nature [17,18]. The free flow of electrons and hydroxide structure of Gd supports as a better co-catalyst [10]. Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel steps are used in the HER process, where hydrogen dissociation and adsorption are critical [13]. Generally, hydroxide bonding with the metals improves the dissociation of water molecules by facilitating initial water adsorption [11]. This study used the advantages of 1D, such as shape, which aids reaction kinetics, low-energy facets, which contribute to nanostructure activity, and defects [19], which are combined with 0D small size, quantum confinement, active surface area, and superior physical and chemical properties [20].

The leaf extract of Wedelia Chinensis (Bhringraj, Manjal Karisalangani in Tamil, a medicinal plant belonging to the Asteraceae family) was chosen as the carbon precursor for the green synthesis of carbon dots in this study. The leaves are used to colour grey hair and to promote hair growth. The juice of the leaves is commonly used to treat Cephalalgia. The leaves include iso-flavonoids, oleanolic acid, saponins, wedelolactone's, and Norwedelolactone (II), which have been extracted from an alcoholic extract of the leaves. It also has mucosal protective properties [21]. Hydrothermal n-CDs were created utilizing Wedelia Chinensis leaves extract and aqueous ammonium hydroxide as a nitrogen source. The produced gadolinium hydroxide is composited with the n-CDs using the reflux method and is represented as n-CDs@Gd(OH)3. The produced composite materials were characterized using standard laboratory techniques, and their HER performance was evaluated.

Wedelia Chinensis leaves have been procured from the local garden at Thiruvarur town, Tamil Nadu, India. Aqueous ammonium hydroxide (25%), Sulphuric acid (H2SO4), Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate, and Sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Double-distilled (DD) water was used to make electrolyte solutions. Sinsil International Private Ltd., Bengaluru, India, has supplied all the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of n-CDs from W. Chinesis extract
ce:section.ce:para: Bhringraj leaves were washed with DD water. The leaves were grounded with a mortar and pistol in 40 mL of DD water, and the extract was filtered. In a traditional hydrothermal method, 48 mL of filtered extract and 2 mL of aqua. NH3 was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-coated autoclave and cooked for 12 h at 180 °C. After reaching room temperature, it was allowed to cool naturally before being filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size filter membrane. The Wedelia Chinensis n-CDs aqueous solution was then filtered and stored in the refrigerator and converted them into powder using freeze drying.

2.256 g of Gd (NO3)3.6H2O and 2.5 M of 15 mL of NaOH were dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was kept for hydrothermal treatment for 24 h at 180 °C. Following centrifugation, the material was washed with water and ethanol. And the finished product was dried for one day at 60 °C.

Aqueous n-CDs solutions of 40 mL and 3 mg of Gd(OH)3 were mixed. The mixture was refluxed in an oil bath at 100 °C for 8 h without any stabilizing agents and constant stirring. It was then centrifuged and washed several times before being dried, and the synthesis process was clearly shown in the schematic representation in the supplementary information (SI) in Fig. S1. Characterization techniques, preparation of the catalyst, and basics of electrochemical measurements were also reported in the supplementary section.

In a standard hydrothermal technique, the green leaf extract is transformed into a brown solution; the n-CDs are filtered and composited with Gd(OH)3, which is confirmed nakedly by transforming brown into black powder. HRTEM images were used to study the well-formed n-CDs surface morphology. The existence of very uniformly dispersed and spherical n-CDs can be seen in Fig. 1 (a and b). The SAED pattern, shown in Fig. 1(a) as inset, clearly demonstrates the amorphous nature of the n-CDs, as well as the particle size distribution of carbon dots, which reveals that the average size of the n-CDs was 2.6 nm, with a diameter range of 1–5 nm [22]. Fig. 1(c) depicts the lattice fringes of carbon dots with an inter-planar spacing of 0.31 nm in the graphitic (002) plane [23]. HRSEM was used to study the morphology of Gd(OH)3 , and it clearly let out the formation of nanorods where the length varies from 300 nm to 450 nm, and the diameter of the rod varies from 30 to 50 nm [24] which is shown in Fig. S4 in the supplementary information. The composite creation of gadolinium hydroxide with n-CDs in the shape of a nano rice-like structure studded with carbon quantum dots is clearly seen in the HRTEM pictures of Fig. 1(d and e). It reveals the carbon quantum dots are ultimately shown across the nano rice-like structure, lattice fringes of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm are marked concerning its zone axis derived from corresponding single atomic diffraction pattern belongs to the Gd(OH)3 hexagonal structure at the (110) and (210) plane and amorphous regions belongs to n-CDs. The synthesized material combines zero- and one-dimensional which provides more active edges for hydrogen ion adsorption towards HER.
The existence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and gadolinium is confirmed by the EDS spectra and elemental composition of the synthesized sample in Fig. 2 .

The XRD picture in Fig. 3 assists in obtaining the necessary confirmation regarding the elements and the crystal structure information. The wide diffraction pattern at 19.5° revealed the existence of carbon at the (002) plane in n-CDs [25] and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, confirming the amorphous nature. In n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 superimposed reflections of amorphous carbon dots at 19.5° and 23.8° are comparable to the spacing of the graphite lattice [26]. The synthesized composite revealed distinctive peaks at 2θ = 16.3°, 28.2°, and 29.7°, which were ascribed to the hexagonal phase's (100), (110), and (101) planes, respectively. Other crystal planes have been given to the corresponding peaks as well. The most prominent peak of Gd(OH)3, particularly at 50.44°, clearly illustrates the synthesis of a nano rice-like structure. The same was true for the HRTEM inter-planar distance results in the same plane [27].

The synthesized n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 binding interaction, electronic structure, and the surface oxidation state was investigated using XPS. Fig. 4 (a and b) shows the full spectra of n-CDs, Gd(OH)3 , and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 clearly evidences the elemental composition of composite contains carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and gadolinium with 67.5%, 23.6%, 8.1%, and 0.8% respectively [28]. C1s spectrum shown in Fig. 5 (a) reveals peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.4 eV, 287.6 eV, and 288.3 eV, which might be attributed to graphitic sp2 CC/C–H, C–N, C–O, CN, CO–N functional groups respectively [29–31]. The existence of pyridinic N, Amino N, Pyrrolic N, and Graphitic N in the synthesized materials is demonstrated by peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.5 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.1 eV in the N 1s spectra [32–34]. In the high-resolution O1s spectra, the existence of HO–CO, C–OH/C–O–C, CO, and H–O–H groups is confirmed by the appearance of peaks at 530.2 eV, 531.2 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.1 eV [35–37] and also O KLL Auger peaks. Peaks at 141.4 eV and 146.6 eV correspond to the 4d signals of Gd(OH)3 respectively [38,39] in Fig. 5(d). It clearly suggests the existence of Gd in the +3 oxidation state. As a result, the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen species on the surface of the composite suggests that n-CDs have been sprinkled over the Gd(OH)3 nano rice-like structure.

In n-CDs and composites, the wide peak in the range of 3200–3400 cm−1 in Fig. 6 corresponds to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [40,41], whereas the acute peak at 3614 cm−1 in Gd(OH)3 revealed the OH stretch. The absorption bands at 2127 cm−1 are thought to be responsible for the production of CH2 stretching bonds [42]. The sharp bands at 1638 cm−1 clearly state the CC stretching and NH bending vibrations in the n-CDs [43,44]. It was slightly displaced to 1608 cm−1 in the composite. Peak 1409 cm−1 was linked to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate anions [45]. C–N stretching mode was responsible for the peak seen at 1314 cm−1 [46]. The distinctive absorption bands of the OH stretching vibration mode appear in n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 at 1073 cm−1 [47,48] and the bending peak appears at 1008 cm−1 [49]. The peak at 627 cm−1 is caused by angular deformations of C–H functional groups [50]. Stretching frequencies of M−O cause a typical Gd=O vibration at 539.8 cm−1 [51] and a peak at 708 cm−1ascribed to O–H bending vibrations which was moved to 779 cm−1 in the composite [27]. This peak shifting demonstrates that metal hydroxide not only has physical absorption but also has some electrostatic interaction with n-CDs.

HER performance of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 has been studied utilizing LSV, Tafel, and EIS measurements in 0.5M H2SO4 employing a three-electrode setup with n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 coated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as working electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. From the polarization curves, the HER performance of the working electrode can be rated at the scan rate of 10 mV/s. The kinetic parameters, such as onset potential and overpotential, were established using LSV curves and must be lower for uncoated glassy carbon and higher for platinum electrodes [52].

Fig. 7 (a) reveals that the onset potential for GCE, n-CDs was −0.457 and −0.360 VRHE while n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 showed −0.0216 VRHE which is near to platinum values. This result indicates that n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 could deliver a better electrocatalytic activity. The value of overpotential of the bare GCE, n-CDs, n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, and bare Pt electrodes measured at −10 mA/cm−2 were found to be −0.524, −0.418, −0.062 and −0.009 VRHE. The outstanding electrocatalytic behaviour of n-CDs@Gd (OH)3 has been observed by these results.

Tafel slope is one of the best electro-kinetic metrics for determining the rate of electrochemical reaction. It is generally known that the small Tafel slope demonstrates a faster mechanism in HER kinetics. Fig. 7(b) depicts the linear part of the cathodic slope, that is Tafel slope of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3, n-CDs, bare GCE, and Pt for their HER process. The computed values of the Tafel slope for n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 was 65 mV/dec, which was much better than the bare GCE and n-CDs slope values of 232 and 172 mV/dec. It could be evidenced as an optimum choice as electrode materials since the slope value was closer to Pt's value of 45 mV/dec. As per the classical theory of hydrogen evolution, the process occurs in two ways: either through Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer Tafel. The slope values for Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel are explicitly stated in theory as 120, 40, and 30 mV/dec [53,54]. In this work, Volmer Heyrovsky is the rate-determining step and put forward that H2 evolution was headed by electron reduction of protons provided by the active sites and the hydrogen desorption electrochemically [55]. The platform for those stages was created by combining zero-dimensional carbon n-CDs and one-dimensional nanorods Gd(OH)3. Both rods and dots are rich in hydroxyl groups, valence ‘f’ orbital electrons of rods, and small quantum size of carbon, which results in a large volume-to-surface area ratio that enhances electronic communication and the chemisorption of hydrogen ions at the surface for catalytic activity. The longitudinal rod shape of Gd(OH)3 canalized the electron easily along its shape and so decreases the electron-hole recombination process, which concurrently boosts its catalytic activity, and the synergistic effect increases the stability [56]. The negative surface charge potential of n-CDs and the signal from the outer electrons attract the positively charged hydrogen ion, which was effectively adsorbed by the n-CDs large surface area, which meets the Volmer-Heyrovsky steps. This electronic surface potential was validated by examining n-CDs with a Zeta potential analyzer, which revealed a negative potential of 25.3 mV, shown in Fig. 8 (a). This is further corroborated by the observation that Gd(OH)3 nanorods have a Zeta potential of −19.9 mV/dec due to the abundance of hydroxyl groups, which promotes the interaction of positive hydrogen ions [56]. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the negative potential of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 has changed from −25.3 mV to −3.76 mV. According to prior reports, the variation in the charge is due to the addition of rods which in turn promotes greater adsorption and enhances the attraction of H+ ions [57]. The typical activity characteristics for catalytic processes are due to nitrogen doping, which causes defects, and the edges of carbon with surface-rich hydroxyl groups and other functional groups, a surface electronic charge which provides electrostatic forces that attract hydrogen ions.

The impact of the work function (WF, ф) on experimental outcomes varies and so cannot be easily generalised from one system to another. For the electrocatalytic process, there is still no good link between oxidation and reduction with WF. Some studies show a decrease in ф, which brings the Fermi level closer to the electrolyte potential, while in other studies, it has been found that an increase in ф significantly aids in effective reduction. When compared to widespread descriptors like shape and atomic structure, the connection between work function and catalysis is, nevertheless, far from being close. In this study, contact potential difference mapping was used to assess the work function of n-CDs using scanning Kelvin probe microscopy (SKP). The n-CDs raster scan pictures in Fig. 9 (a) have an average surface work function of 4.95 eV, which is consistent with our prior findings [12]. The WF of Gd(OH)3 and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 were determined as 5.217 eV and 5.254 eV, respectively. Vallejo et al. and team looked at how to work function and electrocatalytic activity are linearly related [58]. The noble catalyst claims that platinum exhibits the greatest work function with a negatively charged surface and best performance. The work function is likewise displayed by synthesized n-CDs, Gd(OH)3, and n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 in that range and reveals the same in Fig. 9.

Changes in the work function and surface electronic charge potential are caused by the inclusion of Gd(OH)3, which is connected with changes in the electronic structure. XPS was used to reveal the change in electronic structure caused by a 1 eV shift in binding energy of C 1s of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 compared to plain n-CDs shown in Fig. 10 . This rare earth metal hydroxide causes charge redistribution at adjacent nanorods, altering the energy of adsorption and providing exceptional HER activity [59].

Further, the stability of the material is important, chrono-potentiometry was used to analyze the stability of the synthesized n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 for 25 h at a constant current density of −10 mA/cm2. It shows the potential of negligible variation from −0.076 to −0.077 VRHE in Fig. 11 .

This result reveals the longevity and the stableness towards the HER in H2SO4. Doping of nitrogen atom modifies the surface charge of the nearby carbon atoms and increases the electronegativity this provides significant improvement in the durability of catalytic activity [60]. Polarization curves were measured before and after 25 h stability which doesn't show any distinct change.

Impedance spectroscopy is one of the vital techniques to evaluate the mechanisms of surface reactions, charge transfer, kinetics, and mass transport at the interface in the electrolyte. To study the coating effect and kinetics of the materials, AC impedance test was accomplished, and a comparative analysis of the Nyquist plot and Bode plot was carried out and exhibited in the figure at the open circuit of −0.24 VRHE. in 0.5M H2SO4. From Fig. S2(b) given in the supplementary section, the phase angle Bode plot shown for composite materials shows three humps that reflect three-time constants imputed to the transfer of ions and diffusion reaction between the electrode surface and electrolyte, coating effect, hydrophobicity while glassy carbon electrode shows diffusion reaction. Randle circuit is used for fitting the uncoated one, which has solution resistance (RS), diffusion resistance (RD), and capacitance related to the diffusion layer (CD), and an equivalent circuit with six elements are used to fit the experimental circuits of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 which includes solution resistance (RS), coating resistance (RC) and coating capacitance (CC), charge transfer resistance (RCT) and capacitance related to the charge transfer (CCT) and also the hydrophobic surface resistance. The fitted results (shown in Fig. S3) are almost matched with the experimental values, which reveals the 5.29×105 Ohm comparatively very much higher than that of 0.25×104 Ohm, evidenced the high electrical conductivity of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 assures fast electron transport.

The performance of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 as an electrocatalyst was analyzed at different open circuit potential ranging from −0.24, −0.34, −0.44, −0.54, −0.64, and −0.74 VRHE using impedance test. Fig. 12 (a1 and b1) depicts a Nyquist and Bode plot of an impedance spectrum. This demonstrates that a rise in the overpotential reduces the time constant owing to the coating effect in the high frequency area, and also, hydrophobic resistance in the low frequency region is dramatically decreased, as seen by the phase angle Bode plot. This proves that the charge transfer resistance (RCT) plays a major role and in order to analyze the effect fitted results of Nyquist plot with 0.1 V variation, clearly states that the RCT decreases and fine variation in the overpotential also plays an important role that has been clearly visualized with the fitted data and graph shown in Table ST1 (shown in the SI), Fig. 12 (a2 and b2).

The tabulated findings demonstrate the considerable decline in charge transfer resistance from 41.61 to 31.85 Ω/cm2 when the open circuit potential swings from −0.734 to 0–0.741 V, implying a 9.7 Ohm variation in 0.007 V. Likewise, the hydrophobic surface resistance reduces to 0.02 Ω/cm2 from 2 Ω/cm2. These results indicate that faster kinetics of HER by composite towards higher negative potential by enhancing charge transfer which was done by the zero-dimensional n-CDs that provide the high electrochemical surface area ECSA [61]. The tabulated results show the sum of the charge transfer capacitance and coating capacitance is inversely proportional to the total resistance. Resistance reduction implies a capacitance increase [62]. In Table ST1, the value of ‘n’ is lower than one, which indicates that the surface of the catalyst contains some micro-roughness that contributes to some Pseudo-capacitance occurs owing to the adsorption of hydrogen ions on the catalytic surface [63].

Green synthesized, rich surface functional groups n-CDs and one-dimensional gadolinium hydroxide nanorods were prepared using hydrothermal, and the composite was prepared by reflux method. The carbonyl groups, amine groups, especially hydroxyl groups of n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 enhance the surface potential which results in the more electron adsorption in the catalyst surface. These functional groups and the oxidation state of the catalyst were confirmed by the FT-IR and XPS studies. By examining the electron microscopic studies and XRD, the incorporation and sprinkle of graphitic n-CDs on hexagonal Gd(OH)3 were confirmed. The descriptivity factors such as charge potential and work function were measured using Zeta potential and SKP analysis which confirm the change of negative surface potential from −25.3 mV to −3.76 mV and also the change of work function from 4.950 eV to 5.254 eV. The reason behind all the surface changes is due to the change in the binding energy of the C 1s state due to the incorporation of nanorods. The HER performance was tested in 0.5M H2SO4 exposed 0.06 V of overpotential with a Tafel slope of 65 mV/dec using a glassy carbon electrode modified with n-CDs@Gd (OH)3. Drastically charge transfer resistance was reduced which has been revealed by impedance studies. The electrocatalytic studies of n-CDs derived from W. Chinensis supported by 1D gadolinium nanorods flaunts off the enhancement of green hydrogen production in the future and encourage research based on rare earth metals for the same.

Conceptualization, Literature survey, Data Curation, and Formal Analysis, Software, Writing - Original Draft, were performed by Nithya Selvaraju. Resources, validation was done by Yuvaraj Sivalingam. Investigations, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, and Supervision, were performed by Gunasekaran Venugopal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research data policy and data availability statements
ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest. The data sets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are not publicly available because we are working on other applications, but they are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very thankful to the Central University of Tamil Nadu, India for providing an infrastructural, institutional; research facility for this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2023.03.319.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen production from water electrolysis: role of catalysts
Nano Converg

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang S, Lu A, Zhong CJ. Hydrogen production from water electrolysis: role of catalysts. Nano Converg 2021;8:1-23. https://doi.org/10.1186/S40580-021-00254-X/FIGURES/16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Benchmarking nanoparticulate metal oxide electrocatalysts for the alkaline water oxidation reaction †

J Solid State Chem

J Mater Chem
